# Extracao: transcription_mp4_08-Intrapreneurship_Acquisition

- **Fonte:** transcription_mp4_08-Intrapreneurship_Acquisition.txt
- **Tipo:** TXT

---

So far, we've covered a few financing options to acquire a business. In this lesson, I'm going to talk about an alternative way to essentially earn into a business. The goal of this lesson is to talk about how you can become a business owner if you don't have any financing available to you at all. We mentioned this strategy in week one, but let's review the topic again to make absolutely sure the concept is clear. What is an entrepreneur? Let's start by defining what it means to be an entrepreneur. These employees work like entrepreneurs, but they do so as an employee of a company and they work within the constraints within that business. They're critical pieces in the business because they make themselves invaluable by overperforming and outperforming no matter what the role is. These are folks who come in from the ground floor and work their way through all the ranks in the business. They earn their equity over time. I've talked about Oliver Fernandez, for example, where Grant, myself and Oliver bought a business together that was doing $5 million a year for like 30 years. But the person who brought the business to Oliver actually worked in that business for eight years and saw the opportunity. He brought the deal to Oliver. He became a partner with Oliver, Grant and myself for bringing us the deal. And guess what? That person is now running the business every single day. He earned his way into that position because he worked his tail off and brought the opportunity to us, someone who trusted him, and then we acquired the business. This is called earn in, where you bring value to a business and either that business owner recognizes you and gives you equity over time, or when the business owner is willing and ready to exit, you go find a buyer, bring the buyer to the business, do the transaction with the buyer, and now you own equity for bringing the business and running it. Additionally, if you can strike a deal with the business owner and show them that your interest is to grow the business and create massive value, you may be able to peg a certain value of the business that you get at today's value in a future period of time so that you know that when you hit those numbers, you're rewarded with value inside the equity of the business. This would be another way to earn in. Earning in is by being an intrapreneur and either bringing a deal to a third party or becoming invaluable in that organization. Now, I'll give you an example of this. When I started Ottergy Group, I recruited a bunch of kids out of college. For example, my president, right out of college, first job he had outside of selling cars while he put himself through college. Mason started with me day one and I made a promise, if you stay here when I sell the business, I'll give you a piece of the deal. During the course of 15 different years, Mason had eight different bosses. Now there were times where Mason wanted to quit, he didn't like the people he worked for. What I reminded him is that you can always learn even from a bad boss. Learn what you'll never do when you become the boss. So what happened is over those years, Mason elevated above his bosses to one day he was the president of our organization. Now when I sold the business, I didn't want to have to stay and run the business after I sold it. So I made Mason and Misty and a whole bunch of my executives that were intrapreneurs, were helping me build the business from inside. I gave them equity. When the business sold, they made collectively about $14 million. They earned into equity in the business over long periods of time by helping me grow and scale the business. Therefore, when there was a transaction, they got the benefit of ownership in the business. There's many ways to do this, but the point is earn in equity is that you receive equity in the business. In the event there's a sale or transaction because of the contribution you gave to the business or you can eventually, the value of your earning can get applied to the purchase price from a selling business owner so they recognize the value you contributed when they sell you the business. Now imagine you go to a business owner and you say, I'm willing to work five years for you and if we grow the business from $5 million to $10 million, I'd like 35% of that increase. And the business owner says, okay, but here's what we need to do. At the end of that term, if you exercise the option to buy the business, you get a discount from purchase price because the business owner's recognizing the 35% of value you helped create. In addition to earn in equity, if you're a great contributor to the business and the business owner wants to expand, you can get expansion equity. This is where you have great employees who have proven themselves. And then when you want to open a new location into a new market, you give that employee an opportunity to be your on-site partner and you create equity opportunities for those key employees. Now why would you do that? You do it so you can expand at new markets as a business, but you don't have to run everything because you have somebody that's proven to you. You can trust them. They know the business. They know how to replicate and duplicate the business's success. So you give them expansion equity opportunity, which means when they open the new business, hit certain targets and grow it to a certain level, they can earn equity in that new location. And if you use this correctly, you can expand to multiple markets without you as the business owner having to do all the work. In fact, I would challenge you. If you're a business owner and you're watching this, you should be promoting to everyone on your team expansion equity opportunities. This way people don't leave you when they become really good in the business and they can see that they can become an owner as well. Sell ownership as a strategy. Now if you're inside of a business working for a business owner and you want to be a future business owner, present to them. I'll work for you for the next five years. I'll learn all aspects of the business. I'll be trustworthy. I will demonstrate leadership. I will follow your guidance and leadership and help you develop new programs, whatever you need in order to grow the business. What I'm asking for is that when you're ready to expand to a new market, you make me a partner in that market and I'll duplicate what we did in this market. That's called expansion equity. Man, there's no greater leverage for a business owner or a key employee than to harness the horsepower and energy of what you already know works. So acquisition through entrepreneurship is another remarkable way to grow a scale and become a business owner if you don't want to have or don't have access to traditional financing. In the next lesson, we're going to cover some additional alternative sources of financing.
